title_tesim: Ralph Bellamy, actor, with a tennis racket, in resort attire, perhaps in Palm Springs, circa 1933
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Ralph Bellamy
longitude_tesim: Ralph Bellamy's decades-long film career began in the early 1930's. He appeared in many films produced by Columbia Pictures, the first of which was Forbidden in 1932.  In one of the resort images, Bellamy is reading Liam O'Flaherty's "The Martyr," which was published in 1933.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
description_tesim: Ralph Bellamy seated with a tennis racket, in resort attire including light trousers and shoes, no shirt, and with a sweater tied around his neck. He is seated on a low wall of painted brick with a Spanish style building visible across the street.

title_tesim: Inez Courtney, actress, standing on a film set, circa 1934-1939
summary_tesim: Handwritten on negative: [S-2].
Text from negative enclosure: Inez Courtney. Nitrate.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Inez Courtney began her acting career on Broadway before transitioning into films. She appeared in many movies in the 1930s and 1940s, including those produced by Columbia Pictures from 1934-1939.

Inez Courtney standing on a film set. She wears a robe over a collared dress and has her hands on her hips. There is a light at the let, and the stand of another light is visible at the right. There is a chair at the left, and beyond that, a large, curtain-covered window. There is a plant on a shelf above Ms. Courtney, and beyond that an Egyptian painting hangs on the wall.

title_tesim: May Robson, actress, posing in costume
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: May Robson. Nitrate.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Based on other images, Ms. Robson may be dressed as the character of Miss Ashford in Charles Hawtrey's play "The Private Secretary."

May Robson began her career on the stage in 1883, working both on Broadway and with touring companies. In the 1920's she transitioned into films. She continued acting well into her 80's and was nominated for an Oscar for her work in Lady for a Day (1933). She appeared in four films produced by Columbia Pictures, spanning the years 1933-1937.
The text at the bottom of the image is cut off and illegible.

title_tesim: Fred Keating playing a ukulele at his Hollywood Hills house "Casa Escrow" in a publicity photograph for The Captain Hates the Sea, Los Angeles, circa 1934
summary_tesim: On back of photo: [Press tag:] Sweet music on a hillside someplace in Hollywood. Fred Keating, Columbia player, plays to an appreciative audience composed of Aubrey (combination of valet, chauffeur and cook), and "Mr. Neuhmeyer" (familiarly known as "Snoopy"). Fred is one time magician, master of ceremonies, and stage actor (having appeared with Hope Williams in "All Good Americans" and with Talluleh Bankhead in "Forsaking All Others), and now he is under contract to Columbia. His first rile is that of the debonair crook in Lewis Milestone's "The Captain Hates the Sea". Then it is probable that he will do "The Life of  Houdini". [Stamp:] Exclusive. Please Credit William A. Fraker, Columbia Studios. [Handwritten:] File. Photoplay.
Text from negative enclosure: Fred Keating.

summary_tesim: [Press tag:] Sweet music on a hillside someplace in Hollywood. Fred Keating, Columbia player, plays to an appreciative audience composed of Aubrey (combination of valet, chauffeur and cook), and "Mr. Neuhmeyer" (familiarly known as "Snoopy"). Fred is one time magician, master of ceremonies, and stage actor (having appeared with Hope Williams in "All Good Americans," and with Talluleh Bankhead in "Forsaking All Others."), and now he is under contract to Columbia. title_tesim: Grocery store set for Charlie Murray and George Sidney film, circa 1930-1934
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Charlie Murray. George Sidney.
longitude_tesim: The set includes produce, canned goods (Borden's evaporated milk, Willapoint oysters, Monarch grapes, S and W asparagus and salmon), boxes (Lipton's tea, Baby Ruth candy bars, Campbell's soup), and Cudahy's puritan ham and bacon. There is a potbelly stove at the center and there are posters on the walls for Royal gelatin and baking powder, and Mail Pouch tobacco.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Charlie Murray and George Sidney starred in films together in the 1920's and 1930's. They appeared in one full-length film produced by Columbia Pictures: Around the Corner (1930), as well as six comic shorts produced by Columbia Pictures and released in 1934.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Audrey Totter, actress, with husband Dr. Leo Fred on their wedding day, Village Lutheran Church of Westwood, 1952
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Assignment Paris. Acetate.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Audrey Totter, wearing a wedding dress and holding a bouquet of flowers, standing arm-in-arm with her husband, Dr. Leo Fred, wearing a suit, tie and boutonniere. The couple were photographed on their wedding day at the Village Lutheran Church of Westwood.
A similar image, taken on the same occasion, appears as a photo standalone without a title in the Marriage Announcements section of the Los Angeles Times, 21 Sep. 1952: 3.
Audrey Totter famously portrayed the "bad girl" in numerous noir films. She appeared in the Columbia Pictures production, Assignment Paris (1952).
Related ephemera (image arc no. 21198/zz002jd3h9).
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

description_tesim: Actors Colin Tapley and Earl Crawford, seated on horseback, on the set of the western film Arizona (1940). Tapley (seen at foreground) drinks from a wooden bucket with rope handle. title_tesim: Jessie Ralph, actress, caption on back of photo, circa 1933-1934
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Jessie Ralph. Prints.

Text on back of photo: [Typescript:] The spirit of exhilaration as exemplified by Jessie Ralph, character actress under contract to Columbia. Miss Ralph is recognized as one of Broadway’s better troupers and will be co-starred with Richard Cromwell in “Biddy,” a sweet sentimental story of a mother’s love. [Stamp:] Please credit Carl De Voy, portrait artist, Columbia Studios.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: ark:/21198/zz002jdkh2 (front of photo)
Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: It appears that “Biddy,” starring Jessie Ralph and Richard Cromwell, was not produced. The actors never appeared together on film.
Jessie Ralph began as a theater actor, and transitioned into film work in the 1930’s when she was in her 70’s. She appeared in four films produced by Columbia Pictures spanning the years 1933-1934.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Jessie Ralph, actress, caption on back of photo, circa 1933-1934
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Jessie Ralph. Prints.

Text on back of photo: [Typescript:] Jessie Ralph, Columbia character actress, in an excellent pose of a drunken sot made by the portrait artist, Carl De Voy. Miss Ralph is one of Broadway’s best character actresses and is now under Columbia contract. She will co-star with Richard Cromwell in “Biddy,” a sentimental tale of a mother and her son. [Stamped:] Please credit Carl De Voy, portrait artist, Columbia Studios.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: ark:/21198/zz002jdkpn (front of photo)
Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: It appears that “Biddy,” starring Jessie Ralph and Richard Cromwell, was not produced. The actors never appeared together on film.
Jessie Ralph began as a theater actor, and transitioned into film work in the 1930’s when she was in her 70’s. She appeared in four films produced by Columbia Pictures spanning the years 1933-1934.

title_tesim: Constance Towers, singer and actress, in childhood photos, copy prints, [rephotographed] circa 1955

funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Singer and actress Constance "Connie" Towers’ stage and screen career spans six-decades and includes radio work, nightclub performances (supporting and headlining), starring roles in Broadway musicals, guest appearances on episodic television and roles in several television soap operas. She appeared in two Columbia Pictures productions: Bring Your Smile Along (1955) and Fast Forward (1985).

Three copy print photographs of Constance Towers (b. 1933), two including another young girl (likely Lucille Towers, listed with Constance in the 1940 U.S. census). Prints possibly rephotograped circa 1955.

description_tesim: Singer and actress Constance "Connie" Towers’ stage and screen career spans six-decades and includes radio work, nightclub performances (supporting and headlining), starring roles in Broadway musicals, guest appearances on episodic television and roles in several television soap operas. She appeared in two Columbia Pictures productions: Bring Your Smile Along (1955) and Fast Forward (1985).

description_tesim: According to ephemera related to this image (image ark no. 21198/zz002jcw48) this is a copy negative of Harrison Forman's photographs taken in Tibet.

The date, location, and other descriptive information relating to this image are based on metadata from the University of Wisconsin Milwaukee Library's "Tibet - From the Collections of the American Geographical Society Library" digital collection.
Harrison Forman served as a technical advisor for Lost Horizon.

title_tesim: Scene from The Black Knight, copy print, circa 1954

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Copy print photograph from a film still for the Columbia Pictures production The Black Knight (1954). The scene depicts men dressed as knights and flag bearers, riding on horseback through the countryside. The flag bearers wear tunics and greaves (shin protectors). The knights wear bascinet style helmets, tunics, cuisses and greaves (shin protectors), and carry lances and shields.

title_tesim: Sally Eilers, actress, with Harry Joe Brown, director, and their infant son, Harry Joe Brown, Jr., on a bed, 1934
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Sally Eilers
on the back of a photo of this image: [Stamp:] Helen Walter
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: ark:/21198/zz002jfqp1 (back of a photo of this image)
Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Harry Joe Brown directed one Columbia Pictures film, The Squealer (1930), and produced 30 Columbia Pictures films from 1943 to 1967.

The "Helen Walter" referred to on the stamp is probably Helen Walter Root (b. about 1908), who worked as a publicity writer in the film industry in the 1930s and later. She was married to writer Lynn Root.
Sally Eilers appeared in 5 Columbia Pictures films between 1929 and 1951. Her son, Harry Joe Brown, Jr., was born on September 1, 1934.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

summary_tesim: [Press tag:] Elliott Clawson, who began writing for the screen in 1913, has to his credit such screen successes as “The Kaiser, the Beast of Berlin”, “The Phantom of the Opera”, “West of Zanzibar”, “The Road to Mandalay”, “The Leatherneck”, “Skyscraper”, “Sal of Singapore”, “Body and Soul”, “Earthbound”, “Those Who Dance” and “The Little Shepherd of Kingdom Come”. Ince, Goldwyn, Fox, M. G. M., Pathe, are a few of the studios that have been clamoring for his work in recent years. He has written five originals for Bill Boyd and three for Lon Chaney. In collaboration with Howard Higgin, noted writer and director, Clawson is now developing a sea mystery story that will give Jack Holt his greatest starring role. The story is entitled “The 13th Man”.
Text from negative enclosure: Elliott Clawson.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Elliott Clawson did not write any screenplays that were produced by Columbia Pictures, including "The 13th Man." The date of the image is based on the fact that the last of the films mentioned in the text was released in 1929.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: C. Bakaleinikoff, musical director, circa 1933
summary_tesim: On back of a photo of this image: [Press tag:] Constantine [sic] Balkaleinikoff. “Background music” for many of the most important motion pictures produced in Hollywood is composed or arranged by Constantine [sic] Bakaleinikoff, who once directed the Imperial Orchestra in Moscow. He is now attached to the Columbia studios, and scored the currently successful “Cocktail Hour,” as well as the unreleased picture, “Lady for a Day.” Recently he was guest conductor of the Philharmonic Orchestra of Los Angeles, by courtesy of Columbia. . [Stamp:] Please credit Carl De Voy, portrait artist, Columbia Studios.

description_tesim: Marie Schildkraut, who was Joseph Schildkraut's wife, is third from the left. Erna Schildkraut, who was Joseph Schildkraut's mother, sits on a chair in front of the group.

Joseph Schildkraut appeared in dozens of films from the 1920's through the 1960's and won the Oscar for Best Supporting Actor for his role in The Life of Emile Zola (1937). He appeared in one film produced by Columbia Pictures: Sisters Under the Skin (1934).

title_tesim: Ralph Bellamy, actor, with a tennis racket, in resort attire, perhaps in Palm Springs, circa 1933
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Ralph Bellamy
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Ralph Bellamy's decades-long film career began in the early 1930's. He appeared in many films produced by Columbia Pictures, the first of which was Forbidden in 1932.  In one of the resort images, Bellamy is reading Liam O'Flaherty's "The Martyr," which was published in 1933.

Ralph Bellamy holding a tennis racket, in resort attire including light trousers and shoes, no shirt, and with a sweater tied around his neck. He stands in the patio of a painted brick Spanish style building.

title_tesim: Tess Gilbert, script assistant, circa 1933
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Tess Gilbert [crossed out:] script. Portraits G.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This photograph appeared with an  article by Tess Gilbert, "My Beauty Hint," in The Reading Eagle, 22 Feb. 1933: 9.

Tess Gilbert does not appear in the AFI catalog. However, she refers to herself as a screen actress in a 1933 newspaper article and she is identified, in the Brooklyn Daily Eagle 12 Apr. 1936: 43, as a "script girl" for the Capra film "Ladies of Leisure" (1930), produced by Columbia Pictures. She is referred to herself as a script writer for the 1930 Census, which lists her place of birth as Russia.

title_tesim: back of 21198/zz002jg1rv - Frank Capra, director, holding his Academy Award for Best Director for It Happened One Night, verso of print, 1935
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Academy Awards.

[Press tag:] Frank Capra, Hollywood’s No. 1 director, receives the award from the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences for the outstanding directorial achievement of 1934. The Columbia picture, “It Happened One Night”, was also acclaimed the finest production of the year, and its stars, Claudette Colbert and Clark Gable, received awards for their acting. Columbia received eight out of eleven first places on this night of nights!

title_tesim: Lawrence Pohle (probably), writer, circa 1936-1937
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Lawrence Poble [sic, probably "Pohle"]
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Lawrence Pohl was writer for movies and theater. He is credited as a contract writer for the movie Breakfast for Two (1937), a story writer for The Big Shot (1937) and Lets Make a Million (1936) (AFI catalog). He co-wrote three plays which were produced from 1934 to 1941: "Preface to Love" with Thomas Ahearn, "Alley Cat" with Alan Dinehart, and "Wise Girl" with Betty Laidlaw. He also co-wrote two screenplays with Thomas Ahearn that were not produced: "Let's be Candid," and "Take the Heir," the latter purchased by R.-K.-O.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Nina Wilcox Putnam, author, circa 1932
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Nina Wilcox Putnam (writer).
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Nina Wilcox Putnam was an author who occasionally wrote for the movies. Though she did not write any Columbia Pictures films that were produced, according to the Los Angeles Times article, "Woes of Rich Will Be Told" [21 May 1932: A7], Ms. Putnam was hired by Columbia to write the screenplay for the adaptation of "No More Orchids."
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

description_tesim: Left to right (sitting): Rabbi Edgar F. Magnin, Erna Schildkraut, Joseph Schildkraut. Left to right (standing): unidentified man, Marie Schildkraut.

Joseph Schildkraut appeared in dozens of films from the 1920's through the 1960's and won the Oscar for Best Supporting Actor for his role in The Life of Emile Zola (1937). He appeared in one film produced by Columbia Pictures: Sisters Under the Skin (1934).

title_tesim: Press tag describing a scene from The End of the Affair, circa 1955
summary_tesim: Press tag: Cor 3-14. Desperate. “I’ll never leave you, Henry,” sobs Sarah (Deborah Kerr) to her husband (Peter Cushing), although she knows now she can never be with the man she so desperately loves, in this emotional moment from David E. Rose’s “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Miss Kerr, Van Johnson and John Mills, with Peter Cushing heading the top featured cast. Produced by David Lewis and directed by Edward Dmytryk, “The End of the Affair,” which Columbia Pictures is to release world-wide, is based on the best-selling novel by Graham Greene. The screenplay was written by Lenore Coffee. Still by Lawrence [sic] Ridley.
Text from negative enclosure: Deborah Kerr

title_tesim: Deborah Kerr and Van Johnson in The End of the Affair, 1955
summary_tesim: Handwritten on photo: COR3-23A

Text from related press tag: Cor3-23. Realisation. Bendrix (Van Johnson) and Sarah (Deborah Kerr) suddenly realise there is no turning back after their confession of love for each other, in this dramatic scene from David E. Rose’s powerful production of “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Deborah Kerr, Van Johnson and John Mills. Produced by David Lewis and directed by Edward Dmytryk, “The End of the Affair,” which Columbia Pictures is to release world-wide, is based on the best-selling novel by Graham Greene. The screenplay was written by Lenore Coffee. Still by Lawrence [sic] Ridley.
Text from negative enclosure: Deborah Kerr

title_tesim: Consuelo Flowerton, actress and singer, 1930s
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Consuelo Flowerton (mother of Nina Foch)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Consuelo Flowerton was a Ziegfeld Follies showgirl, stage and screen actress, and nightclub performer. She made her film debut in the silent film Camille (Metro Pictures Corporation, 1921) before returning to Broadway stage shows. She appeared in two more films, one long and one short-form, in 1924 and 1928 (neither produced or distributed by Columbia Pictures). Flowerton continued to perform in stage shows throughout the 1930s and early 1940s. These dates also coincide with her appearances as a nightclub performer (she sang and played violin). While Flowerton may not have appeared in a Columbia Pictures film, her daughter, Nina Foch, did. Foch appeared in eighteen Columbia films between 1943 and 1949.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Press tag describing a scene from The End of the Affair, circa 1955
summary_tesim: Press tag: Cor3-8 Dramatic. Sarah Miles (Deborah Kerr), ill and bewildered, is suddenly confronted by her lover, as she is helped by her husband Henry (Peter Cushing), who is ignorant of her changed emotions towards him, in this dramatic moment from David E. Rose’s “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Deborah Kerr, Van Johnson, and John Mills, with Peter Cushing heading the top featured cast. The new Coronado Production, for world-wide release by Columbia Pictures, is produced by David Lewis, and directed by Edward Dmytryk, with David E. Rose as Executive Producer. Lenore Coffee wrote the screen adaptation of this unusual and powerful best-selling Graham Greene story.
Text from negative enclosure: Deborah Kerr

title_tesim: Deborah Kerr and Peter Cushing in The End of the Affair, 1955
summary_tesim: Handwritten on photo: COR3-15A

Text from related press tag: Cor3-15. No Escape. Sarah (Deborah Kerr) suddenly realizes there can be no escape and no happiness for her as she tries to hide the letter telling her husband (Peter Cushing) she is leaving him, in David E. Rose’s “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Miss Kerr, Van Johnson and John Mills, with Peter Cushing heading the top featured cast. Produced by David Lewis and directed by Edward Dmytryk, “The End of the Affair,” which Columbia Pictures is to release world-wide, is based on the best-selling novel by Graham Greene. The screenplay was written by Lenore Coffee. Still by Lawrence [sic] Ridley.
Text from negative enclosure: Deborah Kerr

title_tesim: Press tag describing a scene from The End of the Affair, circa 1955
summary_tesim: Press tag: Cor3-4 Jealousy. Bendrix (Van Johnson) torments himself with jealousy as he questions Sarah (Deborah Kerr), the woman he loves desperately, about her mysterious absence of several days, in this tense moment from David E. Rose’s “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Deborah Kerr, Van Johnson, and John Mills. The new Coronado Production, for world-wide release by Columbia Pictures, is produced by David Lewis, and directed by Edward Dmytryk, with David E. Rose as Executive Producer. Lenore Coffee wrote the screen adaptation of this unusual and powerful best-selling Graham Greene story.

title_tesim: Press tag describing a scene from The End of the Affair, circa 1955
summary_tesim: Press tag: Cor3-P1. Serious Role. Hollywood’s Van Johnson has one of the outstanding roles of his career as Bendrix, tormented, embittered writer who becomes involved in a passionate love affair with a married woman, in David E. Rose’s “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Deborah Kerr, Van Johnson and John Mills. The new role, a complete departure from Van’s previous successes, marks the first time he has appeared before the cameras in the British studio. Produced by David Lewis and directed by Edward Dmytryk, “The End of the Affair,” is based on Graham Greene’s world-famous and best-selling novel of the same name and is to be distributed world-wide by Columbia Pictures. Still by Lawrence [sic] Ridley.
Text from negative enclosure: Deborah Kerr

Rose’s “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Miss Kerr, Van Johnson and John Mills, with Peter Cushing heading the top featured cast. Produced by David Lewis and directed by Edward Dmytryk, “The End of the Affair,” which Columbia Pictures is to release world-wide, is based on the best-selling novel by Graham Greene. The screenplay was written by Lenore Coffee. Still by Lawrence [sic] Ridley.
Text from negative enclosure: Deborah Kerr

title_tesim: Tess Gilbert, script assistant, circa 1933
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Tess Gilbert [crossed out:] script. Portraits G.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Tess Gilbert does not appear in the AFI catalog. However, she refers to herself as a screen actress in "My Beauty Hint," The Reading Eagle, 22 Feb. 1933: 9; and she is identified, in the Brooklyn Daily Eagle 12 Apr. 1936: 43, as a "script girl" for the Capra film "Ladies of Leisure" (1930), produced by Columbia Pictures. She referred to herself as a script writer for the 1930 Census, which lists her place of birth as Russia.

title_tesim: Marion Gering, producer and director of stage and film works, circa 1936-1937
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Marion Gering. Portraits G.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Marion Gering emigrated to the United States from Russia in the mid 1920s and began a career in theatrical production with the Chicago Play Producing Company. He was producing plays in New York City by 1927 and was directing Hollywood films by 1931. He directed two Columbia Pictures productions: Lady of Secrets (1936) and She Married an Artist (1937). With little apparent success, after 1939 he pursued theatre production in Europe, and tried to work in Cuba from 1947 to 1949. He returned briefly to film directing with the musical Sarumba (1950).
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986
